title: Test Your Understanding: Caching
description: Test your knowledge of cache layers, strategies, eviction, and invalidation!

questions.question: What are the two primary benefits of caching?

options.text: Lower latency and reduced load on the source

questions.explanation: A cache serves repeated reads faster (lower latency) and absorbs reads so they never reach the database (reduced load). It does not improve consistency or durability; in fact it weakens consistency.

questions.question: In the cache-aside (lazy loading) pattern, what happens on a cache miss?

options.text: The application loads from the source, then stores the result in the cache
The cache automatically queries the database itself
The CDN serves a default value

questions.explanation: In cache-aside, the application checks the cache, and on a miss it reads from the source and populates the cache. (When the cache itself loads from the source, that's read-through.)

questions.question: Which write strategy keeps the cache always fresh but makes writes slower?
questions.explanation: Write-through updates the cache and the database together synchronously, so the cache is always current at the cost of slower writes. Write-back is fast but risks loss; write-around skips the cache on write.

questions.question: Which write strategy offers the fastest writes but risks losing recent data if the cache fails?
questions.explanation: Write-back writes to the cache immediately and flushes to the database later in batches. It's fast, but data not yet flushed is lost if the cache dies.

questions.question: What does an LRU eviction policy do?

options.text: Evicts the entry accessed the fewest total times
Evicts the entry that hasn't been accessed for the longest time

questions.explanation: LRU (Least Recently Used) drops the entry whose last access is oldest. LFU drops the least frequently accessed entry; TTL expires entries by time.

questions.question: Setting a TTL on cache entries is a useful safety net even when you also invalidate the cache on writes.
questions.explanation: Invalidation logic can be missed or race under concurrency. A TTL bounds how long any staleness can last, so the data self-corrects even if an invalidation is missed.

questions.question: Why is deleting a cache entry on write often preferred over updating it in place?

options.text: It avoids races where concurrent writes leave the cache holding a value matching neither final state; the next read repopulates from the source
Updating in place is not supported by Redis
Deleting is always faster than the database write

questions.explanation: Delete-on-write lets the next read rebuild the entry from the source of truth, which sidesteps some concurrency races that updating-in-place can introduce.

questions.question: What is a cache stampede (thundering herd)?

options.text: The cache server crashing under load
A popular key expiring so many requests miss at once and all hit the database to recompute the same value
Caching personalized data at a shared CDN

questions.explanation: When a hot key expires, concurrent requests all miss and stampede the database to recompute it. Mitigations include single-flight locking, staggered/jittered TTLs, and background early refresh.

questions.question: A cache can be treated as a durable, authoritative source of truth for your data.
questions.explanation: A cache can evict or lose entries at any time (memory pressure, restarts). The database is the source of truth; the cache only holds copies.
